text: Last class both parties signed a valid, binding agreement before a witness.
Now the real test begins: How do you manage the partnership for four seasons?

If the season goes well, we will renew the deal.
If they default again, we will escalate the dispute.
We will renegotiate the fee if the market grows.
Dr. Patussi, we got your notice. We are sorry the payments are late. Our own client paid us late. Please, do not escalate this.
Thank you for calling. I do not want to escalate either. I value the goodwill between us. If you pay on new dates, we will not apply the penalty.
That is very fair. So we will renegotiate the payment dates and sign an amendment?
Exactly. If you honor the new dates, the relationship stays strong. And if this season goes well, we will be happy to discuss a renewal.
We would love that. We will also offer an extension on the exclusivity in Brazil. Thank you for protecting the partnership.
A mutual solution -- that is what good partners do. We will send the amendment today, and we will never need a court.
If the payment is late, we will send a formal notice.
If you pay on new dates, we will not apply the penalty.
Let us renegotiate the dates and protect the goodwill.
If this season goes well, we will renew for two more seasons.
A mutual solution is always better than escalating to court.

Hello again, Doctor Nilo, Sofia here from the FIFA Leadership in Football program office. Last time you read the contract, you fixed the bad clauses, and both parties signed before a witness. The agreement is valid and binding. Congratulations. But here is the truth most people forget: signing is the beginning, not the end. Now you must manage the relationship for four seasons. So here is some advice. First, think about renewals. A renewal is when both sides agree to continue the deal after it ends. The first conditional helps you plan it. You say, if the partnership goes well, we will renew it for two more seasons. If they hit their targets, we will offer an extension. Second, things change, so you renegotiate. To renegotiate means to discuss the terms again and agree on new ones. If the market grows, we will renegotiate the yearly fee. Third, problems happen. Sometimes one side breaks a promise. If a party fails to pay, that is a default. When there is a default, you do not panic, you give notice. Notice is a formal written warning that there is a problem. You say, if you do not pay within thirty days, we will send a formal notice. Fourth, every contract needs a remedy. A remedy is the action that fixes a breach, for example a fine or a repair. If the sponsor breaks the exclusivity, the remedy is a penalty fee. Fifth, if a dispute is serious and you cannot solve it, you escalate it. To escalate means to take the problem to a higher level, to senior managers or to a court. But remember, escalate is the last step, not the first. Always protect the goodwill, the good feeling and trust between the two sides. A mutual solution, one that is good for both parties, is always better than a fight. So, Doctor, sign with care, and manage with care. If you keep the relationship strong, the partnership will last. Good luck.

Let me tell you how we handled a difficult season with one of our sponsors at Corinthians. The contract was signed, valid and binding, but in the second season the sponsor was late with three payments. A late payment is a default, and a default is a problem. Many clubs would shout, threaten, or go straight to court. I did not. I knew that if I attacked them, we would lose the goodwill, and goodwill is hard to rebuild. So first, I sent a polite notice. I wrote, if the payment does not arrive within thirty days, we will have to apply the remedy in the contract. The remedy was a small penalty fee, nothing dramatic. The notice worked. They called me, they explained that their own client had paid them late, and they asked for time. This was a chance to renegotiate, not to fight. So we sat down and changed the payment dates. I said, if you pay on these new dates, we will not apply the penalty, and we will keep the partnership strong. They agreed, and they signed an amendment. Then, because the relationship was good again, something better happened. At the end of the season they came back and said, the partnership has worked well, so we want to renew. We renewed the deal for two more seasons and even agreed an extension on the exclusivity in Brazil. We only escalated nothing, because we never needed to. The lesson is simple. After you sign, you are not a winner and a loser, you are two partners. If a problem comes, give notice, offer a remedy, renegotiate, and protect the goodwill. Escalation and court are the last step, never the first. Manage the relationship well, and a one-season deal becomes a four-season partnership.

Here is how to handle a default and protect the relationship. First, send a formal notice about the problem. Then, offer the remedy from the contract, but stay polite. After that, renegotiate the terms to find a mutual solution. Next, protect the goodwill so the partnership stays strong. Finally, if the season goes well, renew the deal, and only escalate to a court as the last step.
If the payment is late, we will send a formal notice.
If you pay on new dates, we will not apply the penalty.
Let us renegotiate the dates and protect the goodwill.
